news_summary: Daily toothbrushing significantly lowers hospital-acquired pneumonia risk, while meditation effectively harnesses the benefits of a wandering mind.
news_summary_fr: Se brosser les dents quotidiennement réduit considérablement le risque de pneumonie nosocomiale, tandis que la méditation tire efficacement parti des bienfaits d'un esprit vagabond.
news_summary_es: El cepillado diario de dientes reduce significativamente el riesgo de neumonía nosocomial, mientras que la meditación aprovecha eficazmente los beneficios de dejar vagar la mente.

articles.title: Hospital-acquired pneumonia reduced by daily toothbrushing
articles.summary: Most hospital patients don't brush their teeth regularly, but doing so could cut their risk of developing pneumonia during their stay
articles.content:
Most hospital patients don't brush their teeth regularly, but doing so could cut their risk of developing pneumonia during their stay

The benefits of brushing in hospital have been overlooked Drazen Zigic/Getty Images

Brushing your teeth while being treated in hospital can significantly reduce your chances of falling ill with pneumonia.

Many hospitalised patients don’t brush their teeth during their stay, probably for a variety of reasons. Some may have forgotten to bring a toothbrush, while others don’t think about it, feel unmotivated or are physically incapable of doing so. Medical staff often do not include routine oral hygiene care as part of their services to patients.

But in the largest randomised controlled trial of its kind, patients equipped with a toothbrush, toothpaste and dental care advice in hospitals were 60 per cent less likely to develop a common form of hospital-acquired pneumonia, says Brett Mitchell at Avondale University in Australia.

Advertisement

“This just really enforces the need for communication with patients about pneumonia risk, and the importance of oral care and brushing their teeth whilst in hospital,” he says.

It’s widely understood that patients on ventilators often develop pneumonia, due, in part, to the medical equipment interfering with the natural respiratory system. But many non-ventilated hospitalised patients also acquire pneumonia at least 48 hours after hospital admission. Researchers are still trying to understand why this happens and how to prevent it – particularly since hospital-acquired pneumonia is linked to longer hospital stays, higher costs and increased mortality.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

“It’s an important problem,” says Michael Klompas at Harvard University, who was not involved in the study. “Hospital-acquired pneumonia is one of the most common and deadly healthcare-associated infections, and rigorous data on how best to prevent it are sparse.”

Mitchell suspected the disease might be linked to the bacteria in people’s mouths. The oral microbiome can affect respiratory health, as people breathe bacteria-laden droplets into their lungs. And the oral microbiome changes when people are hospitalised, says Mitchell. “I felt it was important that we try to do something to address this.”

So, he and his colleagues developed a year-long randomised controlled trial involving 8870 patients in three Australian hospitals to test the effects of oral care on pneumonia risks. He presents the results of this part of the team’s Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia Prevention (“HAPPEN”) study today at the Congress of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases (ESCMID Global) in Munich, Germany.

Each participating hospital divided its study participants into three groups. None of the groups received any intervention for the first three months of the study. After three months, the patients in one group were provided with toothpaste and a toothbrush, labelled “Brushing teeth helps prevent pneumonia” on one side and “Brush away pneumonia!” on the other. The brushes were designed with a special handle for people with reduced dexterity. Patients also received a QR code linking them to educational materials on the HAPPEN website.

After six months, the second group also received the toothbrushes, and the third group was given toothbrushes after nine months – meaning all study participants had the option of brushing their teeth for the final three months of the study.

As for the healthcare staff, the research team organised oral care training for ward nurses and provided them with links to professional advice on their website. They also encouraged the nurses to remind patients to brush and floss their teeth and to help those who had difficulty doing so themselves.

Outside of the intervention periods, only 15.9 per cent of the patients brushed their teeth once a day. During the intervention periods, 61.5 per cent of the patients attended to their oral care at least once per day – with patients doing so 1.5 times per day on average. Web statistics revealed that both patients and nurses frequently accessed the information on the HAPPEN pages during the intervention periods, says Mitchell.

That coincided with a dramatic drop in the number of cases of non-ventilator-associated hospital-acquired pneumonia, says Mitchell. Specifically, the incidence fell from 1 case per 100 admission days in the control group to 0.41 in the intervention group.

“This study is intriguing,” says Klompas, who highlights the large study size and randomised design. “The message is that brushing teeth while in hospital is not only good for one’s oral hygiene and sense of well-being, but it may also literally be life-saving.”

Pyry Sipilä at the University of Helsinki, Finland, says he appreciates the importance of such a significant risk improvement based on such a simple intervention. “Basically the patients were just provided with toothbrushes, toothpaste and advice,” he says. Even so, results might vary depending on reasons for hospitalisation and the patients’ usual oral hygiene habits.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a large randomized controlled trial demonstrating that providing hospital patients with toothbrushes, toothpaste, and oral care advice significantly reduces the incidence of hospital-acquired pneumonia, a common and deadly infection. This intervention has broad significance for patient health outcomes, hospital costs, and mortality rates, and it offers a simple, scalable preventive measure with clear real-world benefits.
articles.personality_title: Daily toothbrushing in hospital cuts pneumonia risk by 60%
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many patients in hospitals do not brush their teeth regularly. This can happen because they forget, feel too weak, or don’t get help from hospital staff. Pneumonia, a lung infection, often develops in patients during their hospital stay and can be very serious.

**What happened** – A large study in Australia tested if giving patients toothbrushes, toothpaste, and advice about oral care could lower pneumonia cases. The study involved 8,870 patients across three hospitals. Patients who brushed their teeth daily were 60% less likely to get pneumonia not related to ventilators. Nurses were also trained to encourage and assist patients with toothbrushing.

**Impact** – This is important because hospital-acquired pneumonia increases the time patients spend in the hospital, raises medical costs, and can cause death. The study showed that a simple action like daily toothbrushing can greatly reduce pneumonia cases. It also improved patients’ oral hygiene and overall well-being.

**What's next step** – Hospitals may start providing toothbrushes and oral care advice routinely to patients. Further research might explore how these results apply to different types of patients and hospital settings. Training staff to support oral hygiene could become a common practice to prevent infections.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Brushing teeth daily during a hospital stay can cut the chance of getting pneumonia by more than half, offering a simple way to protect patient health.
articles.personality_title_fr: Se brosser les dents chaque jour à l'hôpital réduit de 60 % le risque de pneumonie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de patients à l'hôpital ne se brossent pas régulièrement les dents. Cela peut être dû à l'oubli, à la faiblesse ou au manque d'aide du personnel. La pneumonie, une infection pulmonaire, apparaît souvent pendant le séjour à l'hôpital et peut être grave.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une grande étude en Australie a testé si donner aux patients une brosse à dents, du dentifrice et des conseils pour l'hygiène bucco-dentaire pouvait réduire les cas de pneumonie. L'étude a concerné 8 870 patients dans trois hôpitaux. Ceux qui se brossaient les dents chaque jour avaient 60 % moins de risques de développer une pneumonie non liée aux respirateurs. Les infirmières ont aussi été formées pour encourager et aider les patients.

**Impact** – C’est important car la pneumonie contractée à l'hôpital allonge la durée des séjours, augmente les coûts et peut entraîner la mort. L'étude montre qu'un geste simple comme le brossage des dents peut réduire fortement ces cas. Cela améliore aussi l’hygiène dentaire et le bien-être des patients.

**Prochaines étapes** – Les hôpitaux pourraient commencer à fournir régulièrement brosses à dents et conseils aux patients. D'autres recherches pourront tester ces résultats pour différents patients et contextes. Former le personnel à soutenir cette hygiène pourrait devenir une pratique courante.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Se brosser les dents chaque jour à l'hôpital réduit de plus de moitié le risque de pneumonie, une manière simple de protéger la santé des patients.
articles.personality_title_es: Cepillarse los dientes diariamente en el hospital reduce el riesgo de neumonía en un 60%
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchos pacientes en el hospital no se cepillan los dientes regularmente. Esto puede ser por olvido, debilidad o falta de ayuda del personal. La neumonía, una infección pulmonar, suele aparecer durante la estancia hospitalaria y puede ser grave.

**Qué pasó** – Un gran estudio en Australia probó si dar a los pacientes cepillo de dientes, pasta dental y consejos de higiene oral podía reducir los casos de neumonía. El estudio incluyó a 8,870 pacientes en tres hospitales. Los que se cepillaban los dientes diariamente tenían un 60% menos de riesgo de neumonía no relacionada con ventiladores. También se entrenó a las enfermeras para animar y ayudar a los pacientes.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque la neumonía adquirida en el hospital alarga las estancias, sube los costos y puede causar la muerte. El estudio mostró que un acto sencillo como cepillarse los dientes puede reducir mucho estos casos. Además mejoró la higiene oral y el bienestar de los pacientes.

**Próximo paso** – Los hospitales podrían empezar a dar cepillos y consejos de higiene a los pacientes de forma rutinaria. Se podrían hacer más estudios para ver cómo funcionan estos resultados en diferentes pacientes y hospitales. Capacitar al personal para apoyar la higiene oral podría volverse habitual para prevenir infecciones.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Cepillarse los dientes cada día durante la hospitalización puede reducir más de la mitad el riesgo de neumonía, ofreciendo una forma sencilla de cuidar la salud del paciente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a gentle hospital bed scene symbolized by a large, softly glowing toothbrush and toothpaste standing protectively beside a stylized pair of lungs made from delicate, translucent blue leaves, connected by a subtle swirling ribbon of clean, fresh air flowing between them, all rendered in natural, calming earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: Why your wandering mind is exactly what meditation is for
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Most people who try meditation for the first time expect their mind to go quiet. Instead, it does the opposite: replaying conversations, drafting grocery lists, or wondering whether the oven is still on. This is not failure. According to Kirat Randhawa, a meditation instructor for Alo Moves, the moment […]
The post Why your wandering mind is exactly what meditation is for first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Most people who try meditation for the first time expect their mind to go quiet. Instead, it does the opposite: replaying conversations, drafting grocery lists, or wondering whether the oven is still on. This is not failure. According to Kirat Randhawa, a meditation instructor for Alo Moves, the moment your attention drifts and you bring it back is where the real work lives. “The healing of the practice occurs when the attention is brought back to the breath or the body every time it wanders off. This is where the growth happens,” she says.

Meditation is not the absence of thought. It is the practice of noticing your thoughts and returning, again and again, without judgment. “Meditation is a way of transforming your life from the inside out by transforming your mind,” Randhawa says.

What regular practice actually builds

Over time, consistent sitting builds what Randhawa describes as inner resourcefulness. “We access an internal resourcefulness that can help us meet a wide range of goals, like sleeping better, feeling less stressed and anxious, focusing better, making intuitive decisions, igniting creative flow, noticing how we contribute to our own distress, and learning to trust our own experience,” she says.

The benefits go beyond mood. Research has linked meditation to lower blood pressure and reduced cardiovascular mortality risk. Mindfulness training has also been connected to improved athletic performance, suggesting that a few minutes of stillness before a workout may carry over into it.

A quick guide to the main types

The most common form of meditation is mindfulness. “Mindfulness is the practice of creating a clear, spacious mind through sustained concentration,” Randhawa says. “In turn, this allows for greater behavioral and emotional flexibility, allowing us to think, speak, and move in more desirable ways.” Its instructions are simple enough to begin without a teacher, which makes it the natural starting point for most people.

Vedic meditation is a different approach: typically practiced twice daily for 20 minutes using a personal mantra to reach a state of expanded awareness. Unlike mindfulness, it generally benefits from working with an instructor, at least at the start.

Visualization meditation is exactly what the name suggests. “Visualization is a creative technique that inspires behavioral change by igniting hopeful imagery and emotional expansion through reflection,” Randhawa says. One form, called inner-child meditation, invites you to picture yourself as a younger version of yourself, building self-compassion and creating space to process earlier experiences.

How to start: the basics of a session

A basic mindfulness session does not require much. Find a comfortable but upright position, whether in a chair, on a cushion, or on a stool. Set a timer. Close your eyes and turn your attention to your breathing. If it helps, count your breaths: inhale one, exhale two, inhale three, exhale four. When your mind wanders, which it will, draw your attention back to the breath without criticism. When the timer goes off, take your time returning to the room and notice how you feel.

That is the whole structure. What varies is the duration. Randhawa notes that “completing 10 minutes of meditation daily when the mind is more relaxed and alert is far more beneficial than trying to complete 30 minutes of meditation daily when the mind is distracted.” One minute, done with intention, is a real place to start.

What helps beginners stay with it

Before sitting down, name why you are doing it. An intention gives the session direction. Randhawa describes it as something that “can act as a ‘mover’ of the mind and support close affiliation with internal states that resemble ease, relaxed presence, and alertness.” If you want to decompress after a hard day, say that to yourself before you close your eyes.

From there, the most important variable is timing. “It’s important to identify what our current capacity for mind training is so that we can move in accordance with that truth,” Randhawa says. Early morning, right after coffee, just before bed: what matters is that the time is realistic and protected. A few minutes you actually take will outperform a half-hour you keep postponing.

The harder part is resisting the urge to evaluate. “Recognize that it’s a practice,” Randhawa says. “Just as we would train for months in advance before embarking on a marathon, the same level of precise training is necessary for developing stability of mind.” Sitting down and expecting immediate calm is like signing up for a race and expecting to run it without preparation. The practice is the preparation.

Randhawa also suggests pausing, before or after each session, to acknowledge that you have carved out time for this. “Embodying reverence for the practice, the teachings, and the capacity for developing compassionate awareness invites us to extend appreciation to those who have practiced before and to those who will follow behind,” she says. It is a small shift in orientation, but it tends to change how the session feels.

Finally, keep it flexible. “Let yourself be guided by what your body and mind genuinely need,” Randhawa says. “Perhaps it’s inviting more silence, or it’s inviting some background sound. You might wish to lie down, sit upright, or even try a walking meditation. By resting in a creative space, you’ll be able to show up for yourself in consistent and flexible ways each day.” A practice shaped around your actual life is far more likely to persist than one modeled on someone else’s ideal conditions.

The point is to return. Every time you bring your attention back to your breath, whether it wanders once or a hundred times in a session, the practice is doing what it is supposed to do.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the tangible mental and physical health benefits of meditation, including stress reduction, improved focus, better sleep, and lower cardiovascular risk. It provides detailed, practical guidance on meditation practice, making the benefits accessible to a broad audience. The focus on meditation as a tool for inner growth and well-being has broad positive real-world impact and significance.
articles.personality_title: Why a Wandering Mind Is Exactly What Meditation Needs
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people try meditation expecting their minds to become completely quiet. Instead, they find their thoughts jumping around, like replaying past conversations or making grocery lists. This often makes beginners feel like they are failing.

**What happened** – Meditation teacher Kirat Randhawa explains that this wandering mind is normal and important. The real benefit comes from noticing when your mind drifts and gently bringing your attention back to your breath or body. This repeated process helps build inner strength and calm.

**Impact** – Meditation is not about stopping thoughts but learning to observe them without judgment. Regular practice can improve sleep, reduce stress and anxiety, sharpen focus, and even lower the risk of heart problems. Different types of meditation, like mindfulness or visualization, offer ways to connect with your mind and emotions. This makes meditation a useful tool for many parts of life, from mental health to sports.

**What's next step** – Beginners are encouraged to start with just a few minutes a day, choosing a quiet time that fits their routine. Setting a clear intention before meditating helps keep focus. Over time, practicing with kindness toward yourself and adjusting the style to your needs can make meditation a lasting habit.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Meditation works by training your mind to return to the present moment again and again, making a wandering mind a key part of the process.
articles.personality_title_fr: Pourquoi un esprit qui vagabonde est exactement ce dont la méditation a besoin
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de personnes qui essaient la méditation s'attendent à ce que leur esprit devienne complètement calme. Au lieu de cela, elles constatent que leurs pensées sautent partout, comme rejouer des conversations passées ou faire des listes de courses. Cela fait souvent penser aux débutants qu'ils échouent.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – L'instructrice de méditation Kirat Randhawa explique que cet esprit vagabond est normal et important. Le vrai bénéfice vient du fait de remarquer quand votre esprit s'égare et de ramener doucement votre attention sur votre respiration ou votre corps. Ce processus répété aide à développer une force intérieure et du calme.

**Impact** – La méditation ne consiste pas à arrêter les pensées, mais à apprendre à les observer sans jugement. Une pratique régulière peut améliorer le sommeil, réduire le stress et l'anxiété, améliorer la concentration et même diminuer le risque de problèmes cardiaques. Différents types de méditation, comme la pleine conscience ou la visualisation, permettent de mieux comprendre son esprit et ses émotions. Cela fait de la méditation un outil utile dans de nombreux aspects de la vie, de la santé mentale au sport.

**Prochaine étape** – Les débutants sont encouragés à commencer avec seulement quelques minutes par jour, en choisissant un moment calme qui convient à leur routine. Fixer une intention claire avant de méditer aide à rester concentré. Avec le temps, pratiquer avec bienveillance envers soi-même et adapter le style à ses besoins peut faire de la méditation une habitude durable.

**Phrase clé** – La méditation fonctionne en apprenant à votre esprit à revenir encore et encore au moment présent, faisant de l'esprit vagabond une partie essentielle du processus.
articles.personality_title_es: Por qué una mente errante es justo lo que la meditación necesita
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas que prueban la meditación esperan que su mente se quede completamente en silencio. En cambio, descubren que sus pensamientos saltan de un lado a otro, como repetir conversaciones pasadas o hacer listas de compras. Esto a menudo hace que los principiantes sientan que están fallando.

**Qué pasó** – La instructora de meditación Kirat Randhawa explica que esta mente errante es normal e importante. El verdadero beneficio viene de notar cuando tu mente se distrae y traer suavemente tu atención de vuelta a la respiración o al cuerpo. Este proceso repetido ayuda a construir fuerza interior y calma.

**Impacto** – La meditación no es dejar de pensar, sino aprender a observar los pensamientos sin juzgarlos. La práctica regular puede mejorar el sueño, reducir el estrés y la ansiedad, mejorar la concentración e incluso bajar el riesgo de problemas del corazón. Diferentes tipos de meditación, como la atención plena o la visualización, ofrecen formas de conectar con la mente y las emociones. Esto hace que la meditación sea una herramienta útil en muchas áreas de la vida, desde la salud mental hasta los deportes.

**Próximo paso** – Se recomienda a los principiantes comenzar con solo unos minutos al día, eligiendo un momento tranquilo que se ajuste a su rutina. Establecer una intención clara antes de meditar ayuda a mantener el enfoque. Con el tiempo, practicar con amabilidad hacia uno mismo y adaptar el estilo a las necesidades puede hacer que la meditación sea un hábito duradero.

**Resumen en una frase** – La meditación funciona entrenando tu mente para regresar una y otra vez al momento presente, haciendo de la mente errante una parte clave del proceso.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, softly glowing human silhouette in a meditative seated posture with a gently swirling, translucent stream of thoughts—depicted as delicate, flowing lines and faint, abstract shapes—circling outward and then gracefully returning to a glowing breath at the center, set against a warm, muted earth-toned background evoking calm and inner transformation.
